title: Dog Chest Moving Deeply When Breathing: Causes and Emergency Triage
meta_desc: If your dog is breathing deeply or laboring, it may be a medical emergency. Learn why chest movement matters and when to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Deep chest movement is a sign of severe respiratory distress and is a critical medical emergency requiring immediate veterinary attention. If safe, a brief video of your dog's breathing can help the emergency team prepare for your arrival.

What does it mean if my dog has deep chest movement?

Deep chest movement, often referred to as labored breathing or dyspnea, occurs when a dog must put in significant physical effort to move air into and out of the lungs. Unlike normal, quiet breathing, you may notice the entire ribcage expanding forcefully or the abdomen pumping to help the lungs function. This is a sign that your dog is struggling to maintain proper oxygen levels.

Is deep chest movement in my dog a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. If your dog is showing deep chest movements while at rest, this is a critical situation.
  • Difficulty breathing is always considered a medical emergency because it indicates your pet is not getting enough oxygen.
  • Transport your dog to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.
  • Do not wait for an appointment if you see signs like blue or pale gums, wide-eyed distress, or an extended neck.

How can capturing a video of my dog's breathing help the emergency vet?

  • If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the vet, capture a 10-second video of your dog's breathing.
  • A video allows the triage team to see the exact pattern of the respiratory distress and distinguish between upper and lower airway issues.
  • This helps the clinic prepare necessary oxygen therapy or stabilization equipment before you even walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Observation of the breathing pattern and thoracic auscultation are crucial in determining the underlying cause of deep breathing. Pleural space disease leads to asynchronous breathing, where the chest expands on inspiration as the abdomen is pulled inward, then the chest moves inward on expiration as the abdomen expands. Lung parenchymal disease causes quiet, smooth breathing, with the chest and abdominal wall moving in the same direction, and labored inspiration and expiration. Life-threatening conditions associated with altered breathing patterns include complete large airway obstruction, which manifests as unconsciousness and apnea; partial large airway obstruction, characterized by noisy breathing (stridor or stertor), cyanosis, and anxiety; pneumothorax; alveolar flooding (edema fluid or blood); severe bronchoconstriction with air trapping; and brain-stem pathology affecting ventilation.

Chapter: Respiratory, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1414)

faqs.question: What are common causes of labored breathing in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include congestive heart failure, pneumonia, allergic reactions, trauma, or fluid buildup in the chest cavity.

faqs.question: How can I tell the difference between panting and deep breathing?
faqs.answer: Panting is usually fast and shallow with the tongue out. Deep breathing involves the heavy use of chest and stomach muscles and often occurs even when the dog is not hot or excited.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool, minimize noise to reduce stress, and do not attempt to restrain the dog in a way that puts pressure on their chest or neck.
